title: FAA Laser Regulations Overview
description: Federal Aviation Administration regulations on laser illumination of aircraft.

phases.title: Aviation Safety Context
phases.content: Outdoor laser operations pose unique risks to aviation. A laser illuminated cockpit can temporarily blind pilots during critical phases of flight. Understanding FAA regulations is essential for any outdoor laser show.

phases.title: FAA Laser Regulations
phases.content:
The FAA regulates laser operations that could affect airspace to prevent hazards to aviation.

## Federal Law: 18 USC 39A

### Criminal Offense
- Knowingly aiming laser pointer at aircraft
- Federal felony offense
- Penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment, fines

### Applies to
- Aircraft in flight
- Flight path of aircraft
- Any laser pointer (not just high-power)

## FAA Order 7400.2

### Laser Free Zones (LFZ)
- 2,500 feet around runways (standard approach)
- Beam must not exceed 50 μW/cm²
- Requires FAA approval for any outdoor operation

### Critical Flight Zones (CFZ)
- 10 nautical miles around airport
- 10,000 feet above ground level
- Beam must not exceed 100 μW/cm²

### Sensitive Flight Zones (SFZ)
- Extended areas around airports
- Beam must not exceed 2.5 μW/cm²

## FAA Coordination Process

1. Submit notice to FAA 30+ days before event
2. FAA conducts hazard analysis
3. FAA may issue Letter of Non-Objection
4. File NOTAM before operations

phases.title: FAA Scenario Analysis
phases.content:
Analyze these outdoor show scenarios:

**Scenario 1**: Show 5 miles from airport, beams to 100 feet

**Scenario 2**: Show 1 mile from airport, beams to 500 feet

**Scenario 3**: Show in downtown area with hospital heliport nearby

keyTakeaways: FAA Order 7400.2 governs laser operations near navigable airspace
Pointing a laser at an aircraft is a federal crime under 18 USC 39A
FAA defines Critical Flight Zones (CFZ) and Laser Free Zones (LFZ)
Show operators must coordinate with FAA air traffic control before outdoor shows
